title: I Tested Every Claude Code Feature, These 12 Are the Best
summary: Nate Herk ranks 12 Claude Code features from D to S tier based on 500+ hours of hands-on experience. The ranking prioritizes automation and knowledge work over software engineering, with Skills identified as the top feature.
writeup: After 500-plus hours across Claude Chat, Claude.ai Workspaces (co-work), and Claude Code, the presenter ranks Claude features D-through-S tier by one criterion: impact on daily knowledge work and automation, not on software engineering. The tier list surfaces underused features (custom themes, session recap, agent view, /goal, the Google Workspace CLI) while explaining why co-work rates below Claude Code for power users and why dynamic workflows and Git worktrees land in B tier despite genuine utility. The through-line is a workflow-centric framing: Claude Code is treated as an AI operating system for knowledge workers, not just a coding assistant.
key_points: The ranking criterion is personal productivity impact on knowledge work and automation, not global popularity or technical impressiveness, so software engineers building SaaS products will legitimately disagree with several placements.

Dynamic workflows (parallel multi-agent execution) and /deep research (which automatically invokes a dynamic workflow) both land in B tier because, at the time of recording, they are too new for the presenter to have integrated them into a reliable daily routine despite strong early results.
Git worktrees earn B tier as an important isolation primitive for teams or multi-feature development, allowing isolated copies of a codebase to run concurrently and merge cleanly without collisions.

The Google Workspace CLI rates as an A-tier honorable mention because it gives Claude Code direct, scriptable access to Sheets, Docs, Drive, Gmail, and Calendar, enabling skills built around the CLI rather than fragile screen-scraping or connector UIs.

The /goal command (ranked #12 in the Top 12) lets a user specify a condition-based stopping criterion so Claude keeps working until an objective definition of done is met, making it reliable for fire-and-forget tasks like async research while requiring an objective (not subjective) success condition.

Claude Code's session recap feature, which surfaces a summary of what a terminal tab was doing after an idle period, is called out as a practical but overlooked quality-of-life feature for anyone juggling multiple open terminal sessions simultaneously.

chapters.title: B Tier: High Potential, Not Yet Daily
